vincennes-univ-v-indiana-1852.caseName: Trustees of Vincennes University v. Indiana
vincennes-univ-v-indiana-1852.holding: Congressional school land grants create enforceable trusts; states cannot abridge the trust; enabling acts must be strictly construed according to fiduciary principles and preempt contrary state laws.
vincennes-univ-v-indiana-1852.whyItMatters: The foundational SCOTUS authority on school-land trust formation. The trio of principles announced here — enforceable trust, strict fiduciary construction, federal-grant preemption — runs through every modern trust-lands precedent.

cooper-v-roberts-1855.holding: Admission-act school-land grants are 'sacred' obligations resting on the public faith of the receiving state.
cooper-v-roberts-1855.whyItMatters: Pre-Civil-War SCOTUS authority that supplied the doctrinal floor before Oregon's 1859 admission — and that Lassen (1967) restated in modern fiduciary terms a century later.

lassen-v-arizona-1967.caseName: Lassen v. Arizona ex rel. Arizona Highway Department
lassen-v-arizona-1967.holding: School trust lands must be managed with undivided loyalty to their beneficiaries; full compensation is required when trust lands are used for other public purposes.
lassen-v-arizona-1967.whyItMatters: The modern SCOTUS restatement of school-trust fiduciary law. Cited in nearly every state-supreme-court trust-lands opinion since.

andrus-v-utah-1980.holding: Federal school-land grants are an 'absolute grant' vesting title for a specific purpose; the Enabling Act compact is a 'solemn agreement' between the United States and the receiving state.
andrus-v-utah-1980.whyItMatters: Restates Vincennes and supplies the 'solemn agreement' language that downstream state courts (Kanaly, Pettibone) lean on to characterize the compact.

asarco-v-kadish-1989.holding: Congress cannot impose more onerous conditions on a school-land grant after the trust has vested and the conditions have been met; state taxpayers' generalized economic injury is insufficient for Article III standing in federal court.
asarco-v-kadish-1989.whyItMatters: Two doctrinal threads cross here — the irrevocability of the original compact (favoring the trust) and the standing barrier for taxpayer plaintiffs in federal court (limiting beneficiary enforcement). The standing analysis directly informs the modern OR appellate posture.

united-states-v-new-mexico-1976.holding: Federal reserved water rights extend only to the primary purposes of the federal reservation; ancillary purposes must be supplied by state law.
united-states-v-new-mexico-1976.whyItMatters: Cited in trust-lands water cases for the proposition that the scope of federal reservation doctrine constrains, but does not displace, state trust-land claims to water revenues.

pettibone-v-mt-1985.caseName: Department of State Lands v. Pettibone
pettibone-v-mt-1985.holding: Anyone who acquires an interest in school trust land takes subject to the trust; any restriction on the use of trust land that effectively devalues it cannot be sustained.
pettibone-v-mt-1985.whyItMatters: Margaret Bird's compilation cites Pettibone repeatedly — for access, devaluation, and the proposition that the trust burdens follow the land. Foundational MT precedent.

skamania-v-state-1984.holding: The State violated its fiduciary duties by releasing private timber companies from contracts on school trust lands without adequate consideration.
skamania-v-state-1984.whyItMatters: Washington's leading school-trust breach precedent. Cited as authority that a state release of a private-party trust obligation, without adequate compensation, is itself a breach.

ebke-v-neb-1951.caseName: State ex rel. Ebke v. Board of Educational Lands & Funds
ebke-v-neb-1951.holding: Strict adherence to fiduciary duties is required in administration of school trust lands; policy-based departures from the trust standard are not permitted.
ebke-v-neb-1951.whyItMatters: Nebraska's foundational fiduciary-strictness ruling. The 'no policy override' principle that surfaces in subsequent state opinions from MT, OK, ID.

nigh-v-okla-1982.holding: The Legislature cannot divert school trust revenues to non-school purposes consistent with the trust obligation. The grant-and-acceptance creates an 'irrevocable compact.'
nigh-v-okla-1982.whyItMatters: Oklahoma's anti-diversion ruling. Cited (with Andrus) in Kanaly v. State for the 'irrevocable compact' / 'solemn agreement' / 'contract' triad.

idaho-watersheds-1999.caseName: Idaho Watersheds Project v. State Board of Land Commissioners
idaho-watersheds-1999.holding: Below-market grazing leases on school trust lands violate the trust; the trust requires market-rate consideration.
idaho-watersheds-1999.whyItMatters: Idaho's market-rate-leasing precedent. A clean example of a state supreme court striking down a non-revenue-maximizing leasing practice as a trust breach.

branson-v-romer-1997.caseName: Branson School District RE-82 v. Romer
branson-v-romer-1997.holding: State-constitutional changes affecting school trust lands are constrained by the fiduciary protections owed beneficiaries under the federal enabling-act compact.
branson-v-romer-1997.whyItMatters: Federal district-court authority that state constitutional amendments cannot unilaterally release the trust obligations established by the enabling-act compact.

kanaly-v-state-1985.caseName: Kanaly v. State by and through Janklow
kanaly-v-state-1985.holding: The school-land grant and its acceptance constitute an irrevocable compact, a solemn agreement, and a contract between the receiving state and the United States.
kanaly-v-state-1985.whyItMatters: South Dakota's principal trust-formation precedent. The case explicitly aggregates the SCOTUS authorities (Andrus, Nigh, Platte Valley) into a single triadic formulation.

platte-valley-v-neb-1946.caseName: State v. Platte Valley Public Power & Irrigation Dist.
platte-valley-v-neb-1946.holding: School lands are held in trust under a contractual and constitutional obligation to refrain from disposition or alienation, except as allowed by the Enabling Act and the Constitution.
platte-valley-v-neb-1946.whyItMatters: Pre-Ebke Nebraska authority establishing the contractual character of the trust. Frequently cited together with Ebke for Nebraska's fiduciary-strictness line.

friends-of-wild-swan-2005.caseName: Friends of the Wild Swan v. Department of Natural Resources and Conservation
friends-of-wild-swan-2005.holding: Montana's first Constitution accepted the lands under the Enabling Act recognizing they were held in trust and that the State acted as trustee.
friends-of-wild-swan-2005.whyItMatters: Modern Montana restatement that the trust status of school lands is constitutional, not statutory, and not subject to legislative re-characterization.

responsible-use-v-darkenwald-2005.caseName: Montanans for the Responsible Use of the School Trust v. Darkenwald
responsible-use-v-darkenwald-2005.holding: The Montana Constitution and the Enabling Act impose a trust duty on the State regarding school trust lands.
responsible-use-v-darkenwald-2005.whyItMatters: Montana's standing-supportive precedent for citizen and beneficiary suits against state trustees. Cited in MT ASTL v. State (BDV-2019-1272) standing briefing.

holdner-v-state-2006.holding: Recognizes the State Land Board's fiduciary duties; declined, on the facts presented, to apply the strictest common-law standards.
holdner-v-state-2006.whyItMatters: Oregon Court of Appeals authority that the State Land Board owes fiduciary duties — narrower in application than Margaret Bird and OASTL would later argue is required.

pendleton-v-state-2009.holding: Article VIII, Section 8 of the Oregon Constitution permits declaratory relief regarding legislative funding failures while declining to order injunctive appropriations.
pendleton-v-state-2009.whyItMatters: Oregon Supreme Court's leading Article VIII §8 precedent. The Court of Appeals leaned on Pendleton's posture seventeen years later in the January 28, 2026 OASTL standing ruling.

meinhard-v-salmon-1928.holding: A fiduciary is held to something stricter than the morals of the marketplace — not honesty alone, but the punctilio of an honor the most sensitive.
meinhard-v-salmon-1928.whyItMatters: Cardozo's foundational statement of fiduciary loyalty under American trust law. Quoted constantly in school-trust briefs and in the Library's Reference Desk fiduciary doctrine.

national-parks-v-utah-1990.caseName: National Parks and Conservation Ass'n v. Board of State Lands and Forestry
national-parks-v-utah-1990.holding: NPCA has standing to challenge state trust-land decisions because it raises issues of significant public importance.
national-parks-v-utah-1990.whyItMatters: Utah authority for organizational standing in trust-lands cases on a 'significant public importance' theory — a more permissive standing doctrine than the federal ASARCO line.

sullivan-v-state-2021.holding: Initial pro se trust-mismanagement complaint focused on the State Land Board's handling of the Elliott and the underpriced sale; dismissed below, appealed.
sullivan-v-state-2021.whyItMatters: The first link in the modern Oregon litigation chain that produced the January 28, 2026 standing victory.

sullivan-v-slb-2023.holding: Petition for Review challenging the State Land Board's December 2022 Elliott votes on appraisal grounds; respondent's MTD followed in April 2023.
sullivan-v-slb-2023.whyItMatters: The appraisal-grounds challenge in the modern OR chain.

astl-v-state-2023.holding: Expanded-plaintiff complaint challenging the December 2022 Elliott votes; dismissed February 2024 (Judge Combs) on standing and OTCA notice grounds; appeal produced the January 28, 2026 standing victory.
astl-v-state-2023.whyItMatters: The case whose appeal generated the January 28, 2026 OR Court of Appeals standing precedent.

siuslaw-v-state-2024.caseName: Siuslaw School District 97J et al. v. State
siuslaw-v-state-2024.holding: Filed by school-district plaintiffs; focused on 2024 breaches including the '1000 Road' parcel transfer; First Amended Complaint streamlined to 2024-only breaches; Judge Combs Letter Opinion March 2025.
siuslaw-v-state-2024.whyItMatters: The current live OR trial-court case. School-district plaintiffs is a structurally cleaner standing posture than the 2023 association case.
